sentence: HPI: The patient, a 56-year-old male, presents with difficulty swallowing that has been ongoing for approximately four to five weeks.
He describes a sensation of something being stuck in his throat, which is sometimes painful and occurs when he tries to eat solid foods.
He has never fully choked but has experienced temporary difficulty swallowing solids.
He denies coughing, vomiting blood, or weight loss.
His past medical history includes diabetes and high blood pressure.
He has been compliant with his medications, including metformin 500mg twice daily, and has seen improvement in his blood sugar levels, with an HbA1c of 7.4.
He has not experienced any side effects from metformin.
Review of systems: The patient denies any symptoms in other organs that are relevant to his initial presentation.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of diabetes and high blood pressure.
He has been taking metformin 500mg twice daily for his diabetes.
Throat: No masses or swelling, no redness
Abdominal exam: Epigastric tenderness on the right side, no masses or significant swelling
Blood pressure: 120/50 mmHg (measured in the office)
Assessment: The patient presents with difficulty swallowing due to a possible narrowing of the esophagus, which may be caused by a stricture.
Problem list: Difficulty swallowing; narrowing of the esophagus; diabetes; high blood pressure; unknown status for all other conditions
I prescribed Prilosec 20mg once daily for gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) as it can exacerbate the patient's difficulty swallowing.
I recommended that the patient chew their food frequently, especially hard foods like steak or meat, to avoid getting stuck.
I also ordered a referral for gastroenterology to perform an endoscopy to investigate the cause of the narrowing of the esophagus.
I ordered a hemoglobin A1c, kidney function tests, and an eye referral to ophthalmology to check for diabetic retinopathy.
I continued the patient on metformin and norvasc as previously prescribed.
I will see the patient back in a month to review the results of the blood tests and adjust the treatment plan if necessary.
